title: Automation vs Human Approval Boundaries for Briefing Genie
summary: Automate “preparation” work (drafting, summarization, artifact linking) as safe defaults, but require explicit human approval for any action that changes obligations, blocks work, or could be interpreted as an external/customer commitment. Ensure clear status labeling (draft/recommendation/approved action/blocked) and UI separation between evidence, recommendations, and execution controls to prevent accidental execution or misinterpretation.

claims.text: Automate drafting, summarization, and artifact linking when outputs are clearly marked as AI-generated and intended for human inspection.
Require human approval for policy changes, customer commitments, and release blocking because they change obligations or stop work and need accountable authority.

Any automated message that could be interpreted as a customer commitment must have an accountable human reviewer; internal draft summaries are acceptable only if they cannot be confused with final commitments.
Mislabeling recommendations as decisions can cause operational disruption (e.g., teams pausing rollouts), so explicit status labels are necessary: draft, recommendation, approved action, blocked.
User acceptance improves when evidence, recommendation, and action controls are visually separated; combining recommendation and execution controls increases cancellations and clarification clicks.

openQuestions: What specific action types besides release blocks/policy changes/customer commitments should be categorized as “changes obligations” (e.g., SLA updates, pricing exceptions, security sign-offs)?
Which channels are considered “external messages” vs internal, and what safeguards prevent an internal draft from being forwarded/treated as official?
What is the required approver identity and audit trail format (e.g., role-based approvers, recorded in artifact trail) for each high-risk action category?

What is the minimum UI/UX and API-level enforcement to make it “impossible to confuse” drafts/recommendations with approved commitments (e.g., watermarking, disabled copy-to-external, gated publish flows)?
recommendation: Automate by default: (1) generating draft briefings and summaries, (2) proposing recommendations as non-binding, and (3) linking/collecting supporting artifacts—each with prominent generated-status labeling. Require human approval (recorded in the artifact trail) for: (a) release blocking/unblocking, (b) any policy change, (c) any customer commitment or customer-facing/external message, and (d) any action that changes obligations or halts work. Implement guardrails: enforce status labels (draft/recommendation/approved action/blocked) and separate evidence, recommendation, and action controls so execution cannot be mistaken as implied approval.
